webParts.content:
⚠️ Critical Safety Warnings

DANGER — HIGH PRESSURE SYSTEM

The SS-10 ScaleStick operates under pressures up to 125 PSI. Before performing any maintenance:

  • Verify the system is completely isolated and depressurized (0 PSI gauge confirmation)
  • Wear appropriate PPE: safety glasses, chemical-resistant gloves, and steel-toe boots
  • Open the pressure relief valve BEFORE attempting to remove any housing components
  • Never use power tools for reassembly — hand-tighten only to prevent cross-threading
  • Ensure drainage is positioned to capture residual water discharge

Failure to follow these safety protocols may result in serious injury from high-pressure water discharge, chemical exposure, or equipment damage.

🔧 Required Tools & Materials

  • SS-10 replacement filter cartridge (verify model compatibility)
  • Filter housing wrench (spanner type, included with unit)
  • O-ring lubricant (food-grade silicone, NSF-61 certified)
  • Replacement O-ring gasket (check sizing: 3.5" ID x 4.0" OD)
  • Bucket or drainage pan (minimum 2-gallon capacity)
  • Clean lint-free cloths or shop towels
  • Adjustable wrench (for inlet/outlet fittings if needed)
  • Pressure gauge verification tool (optional, for post-install validation)

webParts.altText: Required tools and materials for SS-10 maintenance

webParts.content:
Phase 1: System Isolation & Depressurization

  1. Close the inlet valve — Rotate the upstream shutoff valve clockwise until fully closed. Verify no water flows from downstream fixtures.
  2. Relieve system pressure — Open the pressure relief button/valve on top of the filter housing. You will hear air escape and see residual water drain.
  3. Open the nearest downstream fixture — This allows remaining system pressure to bleed through. Wait until all water stops flowing.
  4. Verify depressurization — Check the pressure gauge reads 0 PSI. If the unit does not have a built-in gauge, observe that no water sprays when the relief valve is actuated.
  5. Position drainage — Place the bucket or drain pan directly beneath the filter housing to catch residual water when the housing is removed.

Phase 2: Cartridge Extraction

  1. Unscrew the filter housing — Using the spanner wrench, grip the bottom of the clear housing sump and rotate counter-clockwise. Apply steady, even pressure. Do NOT use excessive force.
  2. Remove the housing — Once the housing is free, carefully pull it straight down. Residual water will drain into the bucket.
  3. Extract the old cartridge — Lift the spent cartridge straight up and out of the housing cap/head unit. Note the orientation of the cartridge's flow direction arrow.
  4. Inspect the housing — Clean the interior of the sump housing with a lint-free cloth. Check for cracks, mineral deposits, or discoloration. Replace the housing if damaged.
  5. Inspect the O-ring seat — Examine the O-ring groove on the housing cap for debris or scoring. Clean thoroughly.

Phase 3: New Cartridge Installation & O-Ring Verification

  1. Inspect the new cartridge — Remove it from packaging. Verify the model number matches your system requirements. Check for manufacturing defects.
  2. Remove and inspect the O-ring — Carefully remove the existing O-ring from the housing groove. Inspect for cracking, flattening, or deformation. Replace if ANY wear is visible.
  3. Lubricate the O-ring — Apply a thin, even coat of food-grade silicone lubricant to the O-ring. This ensures a proper seal and makes future removal easier.
  4. Seat the O-ring correctly — Place the O-ring in the groove, ensuring it sits flat without twists or pinches. An improperly seated O-ring is the #1 cause of post-installation leaks.
  5. Insert the new cartridge — Place it into the housing with the flow arrow pointing UP (toward the cap). Ensure it sits firmly on the support post.
  6. Hand-tighten the housing — Thread the sump housing back onto the cap. Tighten by hand only — approximately 1/4 turn past finger-tight. Do NOT use the wrench for tightening.

Phase 4: System Recommissioning

  1. Close the relief valve — Ensure the pressure relief button is in the closed position.
  2. SLOWLY open the inlet valve — Open the upstream shutoff valve no more than 1/4 turn. Allow water to fill the housing gradually. Listen for air escaping from the downstream fixture.
  3. Purge trapped air — Keep the downstream fixture open until water flows steadily with no sputtering or air bubbles (typically 2–3 minutes).
  4. Fully open the inlet valve — Once air is purged, slowly open the valve to its full-open position.
  5. Check for leaks — Inspect all connections: housing-to-cap seal, inlet fitting, outlet fitting, and relief valve. Wipe all surfaces dry and observe for 5 minutes.
  6. Verify system pressure — Check that operating pressure has returned to normal range (typically 40–80 PSI depending on supply).
  7. Record the service — Log the cartridge change date, technician name, pressure readings, and any observations in the Service Records log.
